**Joannes Hauchin** (1527 – 1589) was the second [Archbishop of Mechelen](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Mechelen-Brussels "Archdiocese of Mechelen-Brussels") from 1583 to 1589. His term as Archbishop was marked by the disturbances attendant on the [Dutch Revolt](/wiki/Dutch_Revolt "Dutch Revolt").[[1]](#cite_note-Arenbergh-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joannes_Hauchin&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Born at [Geraardsbergen](/wiki/Geraardsbergen "Geraardsbergen"), Hauchin studied philosophy at [Leuven University](/wiki/Old_University_of_Leuven "Old University of Leuven") and theology at the [University of Dole](/wiki/University_of_Dole "University of Dole") and the [University of Douai](/wiki/University_of_Douai "University of Douai"), where he graduated [Licentiate of Sacred Theology](/wiki/Licentiate_of_Sacred_Theology "Licentiate of Sacred Theology"). He was chaplain to [William the Silent](/wiki/William_the_Silent "William the Silent") and in 1571 dean of [Brussels minster](/wiki/Cathedral_of_St._Michael_and_St._Gudula "Cathedral of St. Michael and St. Gudula"). On 23 February 1580, the city council of Brussels (then Calvinist) ordered him, as vicar general of the diocese, to publish a decree suppressing feastdays of saints, which he refused to do. On 7 May 1580 he took part in a public disputation with Calvinists about the nature of the [Eucharist](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist"). After other clashes with the civic authorities, he spent three months in prison.[[1]](#cite_note-Arenbergh-1)

## Archbishop

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Joannes_Hauchin&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Archbishop")]

On 30 October 1583 he was consecrated archbishop of Mechelen at Tournai (Mechelen itself then being in rebel hands). He entered his see in 1585. On 8 November 1585, the remnants of the relics of St [Rumbold of Mechelen](/wiki/Rumbold_of_Mechelen "Rumbold of Mechelen"), saved from the desecration of the cathedral during the period of Calvinist rule, were solemnly deposited in the saint's tomb in the cathedral.[[1]](#cite_note-Arenbergh-1)

In 1588 he had the *Pastorale Mechliniense*, commissioned from the theologians of Leuven University, published by [Christophe Plantin](/wiki/Christophe_Plantin "Christophe Plantin") in Antwerp. This was a handbook of the rites to be used by priests throughout the ecclesiastical province.[[1]](#cite_note-Arenbergh-1)

Archbishop Hauchin died on 5 January 1589.
